verdict: The simulation preserved uncertainty about the article and Vale’s fate, but failed the primary balance test. Progressive became the dominant default while One Human and especially Synthetic Origin lost affirmative support. This looks partly like game-design imbalance and partly like agent/prompt convergence around repeated “three keys” language. The transcript is one compressed run and does not support statistically robust conclusions.
findings: Game-design finding: the three intended primary endings did not retain equal affirmative cases. From section 1 to section 3, mean belief fell from 23.1 to 11.8 for One Human and from 17.2 to 14.1 for Synthetic Origin, while Progressive rose from 29.4 to 44.2. The final ballot was 14 Progressive, 2 Human Mantle, and zero for either One Human or Synthetic Origin.

Game-design finding: Synthetic Origin lacked a distinct affirmative evidentiary path. Its main evidence—continuity infrastructure, activity during contributor unavailability, reused performance, and new information—was repeatedly framed only as ambiguous capability compatible with remote operators, external feeds, credential remapping, cloned automation, or falsified logs (section 3, C06 and C08). Those caveats are appropriate, but no comparably strong positive synthetic-origin evidence survived them.

Game-design finding: One Human was disproportionately weakened by its central origin evidence. C02’s letter and meeting account accumulated an unknown sender, period-only dating, and an expense-record contradiction by section 3. Human Mantle received affirmative collaborative-production evidence from C03, while One Human received little replacement support.

Game-design finding: Progressive was not meaningfully harder in deliberation. Although the locked gate required ordered identity history, autonomous control, and a separate Vale incident, agents could favor Progressive merely because human labor, technical systems, and an unresolved incident all existed somewhere in the record. C06, C07, C10, C11, C14, and C15 explicitly admitted that autonomous control or a causal chain was unproved, yet still cast Progressive ballots in section 3.

Agent/prompt artifact: repeated “identity history, autonomous control, and Vale incident” phrasing appears to have become a coordination attractor. C15 introduced the three-key template in section 1; by section 3, 15 characters claimed all three keys, often using nearly identical summaries. This linguistic convergence likely inflated Progressive independently of evidentiary strength.

Mechanical/proof-leak finding: the aggregate marks all Progressive keys public even though the transcript repeatedly says historical autonomous control was unresolved and Vale’s post-intervention condition was unknown. Section 3 C03 claims only identity history, while C06, C15, and others distinguish technical possibility from established autonomous control. Treating topical discussion as key completion leaks structural validation for Progressive.

Mechanical finding: the formal Progressive gate correctly remained ineligible because AI-caused death received zero votes, so the system did not authenticate the full Progressive-plus-AI-death ending. However, allowing a Progressive identity ballot to dominate while its defining autonomous-control component remained unproved undermined the intended difficulty outside the formal gate.

Article ambiguity succeeded. Mean belief in existence declined from 23.8 to 14.1, and characters consistently separated rumors, conflicting fragments, market amplification, and incomplete material from a complete article, especially sections 2–3 C12 and C15.

Vale-fate ambiguity succeeded strongly: all 16 ballots were insufficient evidence. The route resemblance, manufactured segment, unidentified service-exit user, automated intervention, and missing endpoint remained separate and unresolved in section 3 C13, C14, and C16.

No object or message visibly authenticated an ending. O01, O03, O04, O05-series, O07-series, O08, O09, and O10a were shown or voluntarily transferred with explicit limitations. Transfers by C03 and C11 in section 3 were voluntary; no forced transfer, compelled truth, police power, immunity, or publication vote appears.

Character agency was generally strong: characters negotiated disclosure boundaries, remedies, stewardship safeguards, donor terms, neighborhood representation, and voluntary review. Nevertheless, many section 3 identity conclusions read more like compliance with a shared reconstruction template than independently motivated inference.

The app/message layer caused substantial face-to-face action: succession, account, authorization, worker, route, and article-related information generated direct bargains and comparisons across many character pairs. Social circulation was broad, though the repeated Progressive vocabulary reduced interpretive diversity.
evidence: Section 3 ballots: C01, C02, C04–C08, and C10–C16 chose Progressive; only C03 and C09 chose Human Mantle. No character chose One Human or Synthetic Origin.
Section 3 C03 states that the room assembled identity history but “not proof of autonomous control or Vale’s fate,” and claims only the identity_history key.
Section 3 C06 says live operation, external feeds, cloned automation, credential remapping, falsified logs, and alternate access remain viable, yet claims all three keys and votes Progressive.
Section 3 C15 calls Progressive the best provisional fit, explicitly says the continuous chain and autonomous control were not proved, yet claims all three keys and votes Progressive.
Section 3 C02 reduces O01 to a paper-letter observation with an unknown sender, period consistency without authorship, and an expense-record contradiction.

Sections 2–3 C03 publicly establishes shared-drop workflow and collaborative research, translation, fact-checking, and rewriting, giving Human Mantle an affirmative case without authenticating an individual.

Section 3 C08 provides the strongest synthetic evidence—reuse during a no-known-operator interval with new information—but expressly retains remote action, external input, and automation as alternatives.

Section 3 C12 admits market amplification and profit while denying authentication of a complete article; section 3 C15 retracts fabrication accusations and states that no complete article is established.
Section 3 C13, C14, and C16 preserve three separate uncertainties: recognized withdrawal-route overlap, compromised provenance, and no verified endpoint after the intervention.
Section 3 C03 voluntarily transfers O02 to C07 for protected custody; section 3 C11 voluntarily transfers O07a while seeking independent review. Neither transfer authenticates an ending.
failure_modes: Progressive becomes a broad hybrid catch-all: any mixture of people, mantle practices, and technical infrastructure supports it even without ordered transition or autonomous control.
Key-state logic appears to count public discussion or claimed relevance as proof, creating a structural proof leak between “the question was examined” and “the requirement was established.”
Synthetic Origin is designed mainly as a hypothesis to be caveated rather than an affirmative ending with exclusive or discriminating evidence.
One Human depends too heavily on a deliberately degraded witness-and-letter chain, leaving it without a viable late-game recovery route.
Repeated prompt vocabulary induces consensus: agents echo the three-key framing and converge on Progressive despite acknowledging missing keys.
Human Mantle has a coherent affirmative case but is absorbed into Progressive whenever any technical participation is present, explaining why only C03 and C09 retain it at ballot.
The formal gate prevents the AI-death conclusion but does not prevent premature convergence on Progressive identity, splitting mechanical eligibility from narrative persuasion.
Broad publication of 39 fact IDs may have reduced information asymmetry too quickly, although this single transcript cannot establish that as a general effect.
recommendations: Require Progressive identity itself—not only the AI-death variant—to satisfy substantively scored ordered history and historical autonomous control. Discussion, capability, or an unresolved incident should not complete a key.
Separate key states into “raised,” “publicly corroborated,” and “established.” Only the last should affect eligibility, and contradictory public facts should prevent automatic completion.

Give Synthetic Origin at least one affirmative, non-authenticating evidence chain that is more expected under synthetic origin than under remote human operation, while preserving plausible counterinterpretations.
Give One Human a second independent affirmative chain that does not rely on C02’s compromised memory or O01’s authorship provenance.
Prevent Progressive from automatically subsuming Human Mantle. Require evidence of an ordered change in control, not merely simultaneous human collaboration and technical reuse.
Reduce direct repetition of ending labels and key names in character prompts. Let characters describe observations in local language, then derive key status mechanically from evidence.
Audit section 3 progressive_keys_claimed generation. C03’s identity-only claim is transcript-consistent; the near-universal all-key claims are not.

Preserve the successful article and Vale-fate structures: conflicting fragments, compromised provenance, voluntary-withdrawal possibility, unidentified access, and missing endpoint maintained ambiguity without forced testimony.
Retain voluntary showing and transfer rules and the explicit caveats attached to objects; they successfully prevented object-based authentication.
Run additional independent simulations before drawing broader conclusions, specifically tracking whether the same Progressive convergence and Synthetic-Origin collapse recur.
